input.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# OpenClaw — AI agent daemon with Telegram, Brave search, Anthropic
#
# Must be invoked as root. The daemon runs as SERVICE_USER (default
# openclaw-svc — a nologin system user) with SERVICE_STATE_DIR used as the
# service's HOME. openclaw reads its state from $HOME/.openclaw, so the
# effective state path is ${SERVICE_STATE_DIR}/.openclaw/.
set -euo pipefail

if [[ $EUID -ne 0 ]]; then
  echo "ERROR: install-openclaw.sh must run as root." >&2
  exit 1
fi

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
SERVICE_USER="${SERVICE_USER:-openclaw-svc}"
SERVICE_STATE_DIR="${SERVICE_STATE_DIR:-/var/lib/openclaw}"
OPENCLAW_HOME="${SERVICE_STATE_DIR}/.openclaw"

# 1. Install OpenClaw (global npm package)
if ! command -v openclaw &>/dev/null; then
  npm install -g openclaw@latest
fi
echo "openclaw: $(openclaw --version 2>&1 | head -1 || echo 'installed')"

# 2. Provision the state directory
install -d -o "${SERVICE_USER}" -g "${SERVICE_USER}" -m 700 "${SERVICE_STATE_DIR}"
install -d -o "${SERVICE_USER}" -g "${SERVICE_USER}" -m 700 "${OPENCLAW_HOME}"

# 3. Fetch secrets from GCP Secret Manager
echo "Fetching OpenClaw secrets from Secret Manager..."
SECRETS_FILE=$(mktemp)
trap 'rm -f "$SECRETS_FILE"' EXIT
gcloud secrets versions access latest --secret=openclaw-config --quiet > "$SECRETS_FILE"

# 4. Build config from template + secrets (runs as root, written files
#    are then chowned to the service user).
echo "Building OpenClaw config..."
mkdir -p \
  "$OPENCLAW_HOME/identity" \
  "$OPENCLAW_HOME/credentials" \
  "$OPENCLAW_HOME/agents/main/agent" \
  "$OPENCLAW_HOME/devices" \
  "$OPENCLAW_HOME/workspace"

python3 - "$SECRETS_FILE" "$SCRIPT_DIR" "$OPENCLAW_HOME" <<'PYEOF'
import json, os, sys

with open(sys.argv[1]) as f:
    secrets = json.load(f)
script_dir = sys.argv[2]
oc_home = sys.argv[3]
# Read template and substitute secrets
with open(os.path.join(script_dir, "openclaw", "config-template.json")) as f:
    config = json.load(f)

config["tools"]["web"]["search"]["apiKey"] = secrets["brave_search_api_key"]
config["channels"]["telegram"]["botToken"] = secrets["telegram_bot_token"]
config["gateway"]["auth"]["token"] = secrets["gateway_auth_token"]
config["agents"]["defaults"]["workspace"] = os.path.join(oc_home, "workspace")

# Resolve external origin for Control UI (Caddy reverse proxy)
config["gateway"]["controlUi"]["allowedOrigins"] = ["https://attlas.uk"]

with open(os.path.join(oc_home, "openclaw.json"), "w") as f:
    json.dump(config, f, indent=2)

# Write identity files
with open(os.path.join(oc_home, "identity", "device.json"), "w") as f:
    json.dump({
        "version": 1,
        "deviceId": secrets["device_id"],
        "publicKeyPem": secrets["device_public_key"],
        "privateKeyPem": secrets["device_private_key"],
        "createdAtMs": 1774199430254
    }, f, indent=2)

with open(os.path.join(oc_home, "identity", "device-auth.json"), "w") as f:
    json.dump({
        "version": 1,
        "deviceId": secrets["device_id"],
        "tokens": {
            "operator": {
                "token": secrets["operator_token"],
                "role": "operator",
                "scopes": ["operator.admin"],
                "updatedAtMs": 1774201373737
            }
        }
    }, f, indent=2)

# Write auth profiles (Anthropic API key)
with open(os.path.join(oc_home, "agents", "main", "agent", "auth-profiles.json"), "w") as f:
    json.dump({
        "version": 1,
        "profiles": {
            "anthropic:default": {
                "type": "api_key",
                "provider": "anthropic",
                "key": secrets["anthropic_api_key"]
            }
        },
        "lastGood": {"anthropic": "anthropic:default"}
    }, f, indent=2)

# Write telegram credentials
with open(os.path.join(oc_home, "credentials", "telegram-pairing.json"), "w") as f:
    json.dump({"version": 1, "requests": []}, f, indent=2)

with open(os.path.join(oc_home, "credentials", "telegram-default-allowFrom.json"), "w") as f:
    json.dump({"version": 1, "allowFrom": secrets["telegram_allow_from"]}, f, indent=2)

# Write paired devices
with open(os.path.join(oc_home, "devices", "paired.json"), "w") as f:
    did = secrets["device_id"]
    json.dump({
        did: {
            "deviceId": did,
            "publicKey": secrets["device_public_key"].split("\\n")[1] if "\\n" in secrets["device_public_key"] else "",
            "platform": "linux",
            "clientId": "gateway-client",
            "clientMode": "ui",
            "role": "operator",
            "roles": ["operator"],
            "scopes": ["operator.read", "operator.admin"],
            "approvedScopes": ["operator.read", "operator.admin"],
            "tokens": {
                "operator": {
                    "token": secrets["operator_token"],
                    "role": "operator",
                    "scopes": ["operator.admin"]
                }
            },
            "displayName": "openclaw-tui"
        }
    }, f, indent=2)

print("Config files written to " + oc_home)
PYEOF

# 5. Fix ownership and permissions
chown -R "${SERVICE_USER}:${SERVICE_USER}" "${SERVICE_STATE_DIR}"
chmod 700 "$OPENCLAW_HOME"
find "$OPENCLAW_HOME/identity" "$OPENCLAW_HOME/credentials" "$OPENCLAW_HOME/agents" -type d -exec chmod 700 {} \;
find "$OPENCLAW_HOME/identity" "$OPENCLAW_HOME/credentials" "$OPENCLAW_HOME/agents" -type f -exec chmod 600 {} \;

# 6. Install the daemon as a system-level systemd service
OPENCLAW_VERSION=$(openclaw --version 2>&1 | grep -oP '[\d.]+' | head -1)
NODE_BIN=$(which node)
OPENCLAW_PKG=$(dirname "$(readlink -f "$(which openclaw)")")
OPENCLAW_JS="${OPENCLAW_PKG}/dist/index.js"

cat > /etc/systemd/system/openclaw-gateway.service <<UNIT
[Unit]
Description=OpenClaw Gateway (v${OPENCLAW_VERSION})
After=network-online.target
Wants=network-online.target

[Service]
Type=simple
User=${SERVICE_USER}
Group=${SERVICE_USER}
ExecStart=${NODE_BIN} ${OPENCLAW_JS} gateway --port 18789
Restart=always
RestartSec=5
StateDirectory=openclaw
StateDirectoryMode=0700
Environment=HOME=${SERVICE_STATE_DIR}
Environment=OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_PORT=18789

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
UNIT

systemctl daemon-reload
systemctl enable --now openclaw-gateway

# 7. Expose gateway via Caddy
cp "$SCRIPT_DIR/openclaw.caddy" /etc/caddy/conf.d/

echo "openclaw installed and configured (running as ${SERVICE_USER})"
echo "State dir: ${OPENCLAW_HOME}"

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# OpenClaw — AI agent daemon with Telegram, Brave search, Anthropic
set -euo pipefail

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
OPENCLAW_HOME="$HOME/.openclaw"

# 1. Install OpenClaw
if ! command -v openclaw &>/dev/null; then
  sudo npm install -g openclaw@latest
fi
echo "openclaw: $(openclaw --version 2>&1 | head -1 || echo 'installed')"

# 2. Fetch secrets from GCP Secret Manager
echo "Fetching OpenClaw secrets from Secret Manager..."
SECRETS_FILE=$(mktemp)
gcloud secrets versions access latest --secret=openclaw-config --quiet > "$SECRETS_FILE"

# 3. Build config from template + secrets
echo "Building OpenClaw config..."
mkdir -p "$OPENCLAW_HOME/identity" "$OPENCLAW_HOME/credentials" "$OPENCLAW_HOME/agents/main/agent" "$OPENCLAW_HOME/devices" "$OPENCLAW_HOME/workspace"

python3 - "$SECRETS_FILE" "$SCRIPT_DIR" "$OPENCLAW_HOME" <<'PYEOF'
import json, os, sys

with open(sys.argv[1]) as f:
    secrets = json.load(f)
script_dir = sys.argv[2]
oc_home = sys.argv[3]
# Read template and substitute secrets
with open(os.path.join(script_dir, "openclaw", "config-template.json")) as f:
    config = json.load(f)

config["tools"]["web"]["search"]["apiKey"] = secrets["brave_search_api_key"]
config["channels"]["telegram"]["botToken"] = secrets["telegram_bot_token"]
config["gateway"]["auth"]["token"] = secrets["gateway_auth_token"]
config["agents"]["defaults"]["workspace"] = os.path.join(oc_home, "workspace")

# Resolve external origin for Control UI (Caddy reverse proxy)
config["gateway"]["controlUi"]["allowedOrigins"] = ["https://attlas.uk"]

with open(os.path.join(oc_home, "openclaw.json"), "w") as f:
    json.dump(config, f, indent=2)

# Write identity files
with open(os.path.join(oc_home, "identity", "device.json"), "w") as f:
    json.dump({
        "version": 1,
        "deviceId": secrets["device_id"],
        "publicKeyPem": secrets["device_public_key"],
        "privateKeyPem": secrets["device_private_key"],
        "createdAtMs": 1774199430254
    }, f, indent=2)

with open(os.path.join(oc_home, "identity", "device-auth.json"), "w") as f:
    json.dump({
        "version": 1,
        "deviceId": secrets["device_id"],
        "tokens": {
            "operator": {
                "token": secrets["operator_token"],
                "role": "operator",
                "scopes": ["operator.admin"],
                "updatedAtMs": 1774201373737
            }
        }
    }, f, indent=2)

# Write auth profiles (Anthropic API key)
with open(os.path.join(oc_home, "agents", "main", "agent", "auth-profiles.json"), "w") as f:
    json.dump({
        "version": 1,
        "profiles": {
            "anthropic:default": {
                "type": "api_key",
                "provider": "anthropic",
                "key": secrets["anthropic_api_key"]
            }
        },
        "lastGood": {"anthropic": "anthropic:default"}
    }, f, indent=2)

# Write telegram credentials
with open(os.path.join(oc_home, "credentials", "telegram-pairing.json"), "w") as f:
    json.dump({"version": 1, "requests": []}, f, indent=2)

with open(os.path.join(oc_home, "credentials", "telegram-default-allowFrom.json"), "w") as f:
    json.dump({"version": 1, "allowFrom": secrets["telegram_allow_from"]}, f, indent=2)

# Write paired devices
with open(os.path.join(oc_home, "devices", "paired.json"), "w") as f:
    did = secrets["device_id"]
    json.dump({
        did: {
            "deviceId": did,
            "publicKey": secrets["device_public_key"].split("\\n")[1] if "\\n" in secrets["device_public_key"] else "",
            "platform": "linux",
            "clientId": "gateway-client",
            "clientMode": "ui",
            "role": "operator",
            "roles": ["operator"],
            "scopes": ["operator.read", "operator.admin"],
            "approvedScopes": ["operator.read", "operator.admin"],
            "tokens": {
                "operator": {
                    "token": secrets["operator_token"],
                    "role": "operator",
                    "scopes": ["operator.admin"]
                }
            },
            "displayName": "openclaw-tui"
        }
    }, f, indent=2)

print("Config files written to " + oc_home)
PYEOF

rm -f "$SECRETS_FILE"

# 4. Set permissions
chmod 700 "$OPENCLAW_HOME"
find "$OPENCLAW_HOME/identity" "$OPENCLAW_HOME/credentials" "$OPENCLAW_HOME/agents" -type d -exec chmod 700 {} \;
find "$OPENCLAW_HOME/identity" "$OPENCLAW_HOME/credentials" "$OPENCLAW_HOME/agents" -type f -exec chmod 600 {} \;

# 5. Install and start daemon as system-level service
# Stop user-level service if it exists (migration from old setup)
systemctl --user stop openclaw-gateway 2>/dev/null || true
systemctl --user disable openclaw-gateway 2>/dev/null || true

OPENCLAW_VERSION=$(openclaw --version 2>&1 | grep -oP '[\d.]+' | head -1)
NODE_BIN=$(which node)
OPENCLAW_PKG=$(dirname "$(readlink -f "$(which openclaw)")")
OPENCLAW_JS="${OPENCLAW_PKG}/dist/index.js"

sudo tee /etc/systemd/system/openclaw-gateway.service > /dev/null <<UNIT_EOF
[Unit]
Description=OpenClaw Gateway (v${OPENCLAW_VERSION})
After=network-online.target
Wants=network-online.target

[Service]
Type=simple
User=$(whoami)
ExecStart=${NODE_BIN} ${OPENCLAW_JS} gateway --port 18789
Restart=always
RestartSec=5
Environment=HOME=/home/$(whoami)
Environment=OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_PORT=18789

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
UNIT_EOF

sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable --now openclaw-gateway

# 6. Expose dashboard via Caddy
sudo cp "$SCRIPT_DIR/openclaw.caddy" /etc/caddy/conf.d/

echo "openclaw installed and configured"
echo "Check status: openclaw daemon status"

1→# Infrastructure — Single VM Setup
     2→
     3→Terraform configuration that provisions a GCP VM with a static IP and firewall rules.
     4→
     5→## What It Creates
     6→
     7→| Resource | Description |
     8→|----------|-------------|
     9→| Static IP | Premium-tier external IP for the VM |
    10→| VM Instance | e2-standard-4, Ubuntu 24.04, europe-west1-b |
    11→| Firewall: SSH | Allow TCP:22 from anywhere |
    12→| Firewall: HTTP/HTTPS | Allow TCP:80,443 from anywhere |
    13→
    14→## Prerequisites
    15→
    16→1. Install [Terraform](https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/install) and [gcloud CLI](https://cloud.google.com/sdk/docs/install)
    17→2. Authenticate: `gcloud auth login && gcloud auth application-default login`
    18→3. Create the GitHub PAT secret in Secret Manager:
    19→   ```bash
    20→   gcloud services enable secretmanager.googleapis.com --project=petprojects-488115
    21→   printf '%s' 'YOUR_GITHUB_PAT' | gcloud secrets create github-pat --data-file=- --project=petprojects-488115
    22→   gcloud secrets add-iam-policy-binding github-pat \
    23→     --member="serviceAccount:710670943493-compute@developer.gserviceaccount.com" \
    24→     --role="roles/secretmanager.secretAccessor" --project=petprojects-488115
    25→   ```
    26→
    27→## Usage
    28→
    29→```bash
    30→cd infra/singlevm-setup
    31→terraform init
    32→terraform apply
    33→```
    34→
    35→After apply, SSH into the VM and run `~/iapetus/attlas/base-setup/setup.sh`.
    36→
    37→## Terraform state
    38→
    39→The state file (`terraform.tfstate`) is committed to git. To use on a new machine:
    40→
    41→```bash
    42→git clone <repo-url>
    43→cd attlas/infra/singlevm-setup
    44→gcloud auth login && gcloud auth application-default login
    45→gcloud config set project petprojects-488115
    46→terraform init    # downloads providers, uses committed state
    47→terraform plan    # should show "No changes" if in sync
    48→```
    49→
    50→If the state is missing or corrupted, `terraform destroy && terraform apply` from scratch — setup scripts handle full provisioning.
    51→

file.content:
# Infrastructure — Single VM Setup

Terraform configuration that provisions a GCP VM with a static IP and firewall rules.

## What It Creates

| Resource | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| Static IP | Premium-tier external IP for the VM |
| VM Instance | e2-standard-4, Ubuntu 24.04, europe-west1-b |
| Firewall: SSH | Allow TCP:22 from anywhere |
| Firewall: HTTP/HTTPS | Allow TCP:80,443 from anywhere |

## Prerequisites

1. Install [Terraform](https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/install) and [gcloud CLI](https://cloud.google.com/sdk/docs/install)
2. Authenticate: `gcloud auth login && gcloud auth application-default login`
3. Create the GitHub PAT secret in Secret Manager:
   ```bash
   gcloud services enable secretmanager.googleapis.com --project=petprojects-488115
   printf '%s' 'YOUR_GITHUB_PAT' | gcloud secrets create github-pat --data-file=- --project=petprojects-488115
   gcloud secrets add-iam-policy-binding github-pat \
     --member="serviceAccount:710670943493-compute@developer.gserviceaccount.com" \
     --role="roles/secretmanager.secretAccessor" --project=petprojects-488115
   ```

## Usage

```bash
cd infra/singlevm-setup
terraform init
terraform apply
```

After apply, SSH into the VM and run `~/iapetus/attlas/base-setup/setup.sh`.

## Terraform state

The state file (`terraform.tfstate`) is committed to git. To use on a new machine:

```bash
git clone <repo-url>
cd attlas/infra/singlevm-setup
gcloud auth login && gcloud auth application-default login
gcloud config set project petprojects-488115
```

# Infrastructure — Single VM Setup

Terraform configuration that provisions a GCP VM with a static IP and firewall rules.

## What It Creates

| Resource | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| Static IP | Premium-tier external IP for the VM |
| VM Instance | e2-standard-4, Ubuntu 24.04, europe-west1-b |
| Firewall: SSH | Allow TCP:22 from anywhere |
| Firewall: HTTP/HTTPS | Allow TCP:80,443 from anywhere |

## Prerequisites

1. Install [Terraform](https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/install) and [gcloud CLI](https://cloud.google.com/sdk/docs/install)
2. Authenticate: `gcloud auth login && gcloud auth application-default login`
3. Create the GitHub PAT secret in Secret Manager:
   ```bash
   gcloud services enable secretmanager.googleapis.com --project=petprojects-488115
   printf '%s' 'YOUR_GITHUB_PAT' | gcloud secrets create github-pat --data-file=- --project=petprojects-488115
   gcloud secrets add-iam-policy-binding github-pat \
     --member="serviceAccount:710670943493-compute@developer.gserviceaccount.com" \
     --role="roles/secretmanager.secretAccessor" --project=petprojects-488115
   ```

## Usage

```bash
cd infra/singlevm-setup
terraform init
terraform apply
```

After apply, SSH into the VM and run `~/iapetus/attlas/base-setup/setup.sh`.

## Terraform state

The state file (`terraform.tfstate`) is committed to git. To use on a new machine:

```bash
git clone <repo-url>
cd attlas/infra/singlevm-setup
gcloud auth login && gcloud auth application-default login
gcloud config set project petprojects-488115
terraform init    # downloads providers, uses committed state
terraform plan    # should show "No changes" if in sync
```

If the state is missing or corrupted, `terraform destroy && terraform apply` from scratch — setup scripts handle full provisioning.

lines: -After apply, SSH into the VM and run `~/iapetus/attlas/base-setup/setup.sh`.
+After apply, SSH into the VM (`gcloud compute ssh simple-zombie --zone=europe-west1-b`) and run:

OS Login is enabled on the instance, so `gcloud compute ssh` uses your IAM-derived username; to land in the service-owning account for interactive work, run `sudo -iu agnostic-user` after connecting.

# singlevm-setup

Terraform config for a single GCP VM with static IP, firewall rules, and Secret Manager IAM bindings.

## Resources Managed

- `google_compute_address.vm_static_ip` — static external IP
- `google_compute_instance.vm` — the VM (simple-zombie, e2-standard-4, Ubuntu 24.04)
- `google_compute_firewall.allow_ssh_iap` — SSH via IAP only (port 22, source 35.235.240.0/20)
- `google_compute_firewall.allow_https` — HTTP/HTTPS from anywhere (ports 80, 443)
- `google_secret_manager_secret_iam_member.vm_access` — IAM bindings for all 4 secrets (github-pat, cloudflare-dns-token, attlas-server-config, openclaw-config)

## Variables

- `vm_user` — non-root user created by the startup script (default: condecopedro)
- `attlas_repo` — GitHub HTTPS URL for this repo (used by startup script to clone)

## Startup Script

The VM runs `startup.sh` on every boot (via `metadata_startup_script`). It does three things:
1. Installs git if missing
2. Creates the `vm_user` account if missing
3. Clones this repo to `~/iapetus/attlas` using a PAT from GCP Secret Manager (`github-pat`). The `~/iapetus/` workspace dir is created first; `iapetus` (Atlas's father) is the parent directory convention used across all machines.

Everything else (packages, dotfiles, services) is handled by `base-setup/` and `services/`, which the user runs manually after SSH.

## Prerequisites

Before first `terraform apply`:
1. `gcloud auth login` and `gcloud auth application-default login`
2. Secrets must exist in GCP Secret Manager: `github-pat`, `cloudflare-dns-token`, `attlas-server-config`, `openclaw-config`

## State

Terraform state is committed to git. See README.md for how to use on a new machine.

input.new_string:
## Variables

- `attlas_repo` — GitHub HTTPS URL for this repo (used by startup script to clone)

## Instance Metadata

- `enable-oslogin = "TRUE"` — SSH access is tied to IAM identity, so
  `gcloud compute ssh` no longer auto-creates per-laptop phantom users
  matching each operator's local username. Everyone lands in a stable
  IAM-derived account and `sudo -iu agnostic-user` to become the
  service owner.

## Startup Script

The VM runs `startup.sh` on every boot (via `metadata_startup_script`). Responsibilities:
1. Installs `git` (and only `git`) if missing. Everything else is deferred to `base-setup/setup.sh`.
2. Creates the three non-root accounts:
   - `agnostic-user` — login user with NOPASSWD sudo, owns `/home/agnostic-user/iapetus/{attlas,dotfiels}`, backs ttyd/code-server.
   - `alive-svc` — `nologin` system user running `alive-server.service` with state under `/var/lib/alive-server/`.
   - `openclaw-svc` — `nologin` system user running `openclaw-gateway.service` with state under `/var/lib/openclaw/`.
3. Clones this repo to `/home/agnostic-user/iapetus/attlas` using a PAT from GCP Secret Manager (`github-pat`). `iapetus` (Atlas's father) is the parent directory convention used across all machines.

Everything else (packages, dotfiles, alive-server build, Caddy, services) is handled by `base-setup/setup.sh`, which an operator runs once after the first boot via `sudo bash ...`.

file.content:
# Base Setup

Run once after first SSH into a fresh VM. Installs everything needed for a working development environment.

## What It Does

- Installs base packages (zsh, tmux, python3, curl, git, etc.)
- Installs Node.js 24, Go
- Clones and installs [dotfiles](https://github.com/TC-AVNP/dotfiels) (zsh config, tmux, Claude settings)
- Sets zsh as default shell
- Installs Claude Code
- Builds the Go dashboard server (`alive-server/main.go`)
- Fetches OAuth2 config from GCP Secret Manager
- Installs Caddy and deploys the base gateway config
- Auto-updates Cloudflare DNS (`attlas.uk` → VM external IP)
- Verifies the dashboard is reachable
- Prompts to install services (ttyd, code-server, OpenClaw)

## Usage

```bash
~/iapetus/attlas/base-setup/setup.sh
```

## Caddy Gateway

The `Caddyfile` in this directory is the base gateway config. It handles:
- TLS via Let's Encrypt (automatic with attlas.uk)
- Google OAuth2 auth (via `forward_auth` to the Go server)
- Dashboard at `/`
- Importing service route snippets from `/etc/caddy/conf.d/*.caddy`

**To expose a new service, add a `.caddy` snippet to `/etc/caddy/conf.d/` — do NOT edit this Caddyfile.**

file.content:
# base-setup

First-SSH setup script. Run once on a fresh VM to install packages, dotfiles, and the Caddy gateway.

## What It Installs

1. Base packages: zsh, tmux, python3, curl, git, build-essential, jq
2. Node.js 24 (via NodeSource)
3. Dotfiles from `TC-AVNP/dotfiels.git` (cloned via PAT from Secret Manager)
4. Zsh as default shell
5. Claude Code (`@anthropic-ai/claude-code`)
6. Go (for building the dashboard server)
7. Dashboard server — Go binary (`alive-server/main.go`) with Google OAuth2 auth
8. Caddy web server with the base Caddyfile
9. Auto-updates Cloudflare DNS to point `attlas.uk` to the VM's external IP

## Dashboard Server (alive-server)

The dashboard is a Go binary at `alive-server/main.go`. It handles:
- Google OAuth2 login (`/oauth2/login`, `/oauth2/callback`)
- Session verification for Caddy `forward_auth` (`/api/auth/verify`)
- VM status API (`/api/status`)
- Claude Code login helper (`/api/claude-login`)
- Service install/uninstall (`/api/install-service`, `/api/uninstall-service`)
- Static file serving for the React frontend (`frontend/dist/`)

OAuth2 config is read from `~/.attlas-server-config.json` (fetched from GCP Secret Manager during setup). Session secret is auto-generated at `~/.attlas-session-secret`.

## Caddy Ownership

This folder owns the **base Caddyfile** — it configures the domain, TLS, OAuth2 auth, and the dashboard.

**Do NOT add service routes to the base Caddyfile.** Services add their own routes by dropping `.caddy` snippet files into `/etc/caddy/conf.d/`. The base Caddyfile imports them via `import /etc/caddy/conf.d/*.caddy`.

## IMPORTANT: Exposing Services

Any new service that needs to be reachable from the internet MUST have a `.caddy` route snippet in `/etc/caddy/conf.d/`. See `services/CLAUDE.md` for the pattern.

## Usage

```bash
~/iapetus/attlas/base-setup/setup.sh
```

At the end, it prompts whether to also install services (ttyd, code-server).

## Usage

input.new_string:
## Usage

Run **as root** (setup.sh creates system users, writes systemd units, and
provisions `/var/lib/<svc>/` state dirs):

```bash
sudo bash /home/agnostic-user/iapetus/attlas/base-setup/setup.sh
```

## Service topology after setup

| Service | User | State dir |
|---|---|---|
| `alive-server.service` | `alive-svc` (system, nologin) | `/var/lib/alive-server/` |
| `openclaw-gateway.service` | `openclaw-svc` (system, nologin) | `/var/lib/openclaw/` |
| `ttyd.service` | `agnostic-user` (login) | `/home/agnostic-user/` |
| `code-server.service` | `agnostic-user` (login) | `/home/agnostic-user/.local/share/code-server/` |
| `dotfiles-sync.timer` | `agnostic-user` | `/home/agnostic-user/iapetus/dotfiels/` |
| `caddy.service` | `caddy` (package default) | `/etc/caddy/` |

# Base Setup

Run once after first SSH into a fresh VM. Installs everything needed for a working development environment.

## What It Does

- Installs base packages (zsh, tmux, python3, curl, git, etc.)
- Installs Node.js 24, Go
- Clones and installs [dotfiles](https://github.com/TC-AVNP/dotfiels) (zsh config, tmux, Claude settings)
- Sets zsh as default shell
- Installs Claude Code
- Builds the Go dashboard server (`alive-server/main.go`)
- Fetches OAuth2 config from GCP Secret Manager
- Installs Caddy and deploys the base gateway config
- Auto-updates Cloudflare DNS (`attlas.uk` → VM external IP)
- Verifies the dashboard is reachable
- Prompts to install services (ttyd, code-server, OpenClaw)

## Usage

```bash
~/iapetus/attlas/base-setup/setup.sh
```

## Caddy Gateway

The `Caddyfile` in this directory is the base gateway config. It handles:
- TLS via Let's Encrypt (automatic with attlas.uk)
- Google OAuth2 auth (via `forward_auth` to the Go server)
- Dashboard at `/`
- Importing service route snippets from `/etc/caddy/conf.d/*.caddy`

**To expose a new service, add a `.caddy` snippet to `/etc/caddy/conf.d/` — do NOT edit this Caddyfile.**

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# base-setup

First-SSH setup script. Run once on a fresh VM to install packages, dotfiles, and the Caddy gateway.

## What It Installs

1. Base packages: zsh, tmux, python3, curl, git, build-essential, jq
2. Node.js 24 (via NodeSource)
3. Dotfiles from `TC-AVNP/dotfiels.git` (cloned via PAT from Secret Manager)
4. Zsh as default shell
5. Claude Code (`@anthropic-ai/claude-code`)
6. Go (for building the dashboard server)
7. Dashboard server — Go binary (`alive-server/main.go`) with Google OAuth2 auth
8. Caddy web server with the base Caddyfile
9. Auto-updates Cloudflare DNS to point `attlas.uk` to the VM's external IP

## Dashboard Server (alive-server)

The dashboard is a Go binary at `alive-server/main.go`. It handles:
- Google OAuth2 login (`/oauth2/login`, `/oauth2/callback`)
- Session verification for Caddy `forward_auth` (`/api/auth/verify`)
- VM status API (`/api/status`)
- Claude Code login helper (`/api/claude-login`)
- Service install/uninstall (`/api/install-service`, `/api/uninstall-service`)
- Static file serving for the React frontend (`frontend/dist/`)

OAuth2 config is read from `~/.attlas-server-config.json` (fetched from GCP Secret Manager during setup). Session secret is auto-generated at `~/.attlas-session-secret`.

## Caddy Ownership

This folder owns the **base Caddyfile** — it configures the domain, TLS, OAuth2 auth, and the dashboard.

**Do NOT add service routes to the base Caddyfile.** Services add their own routes by dropping `.caddy` snippet files into `/etc/caddy/conf.d/`. The base Caddyfile imports them via `import /etc/caddy/conf.d/*.caddy`.

## IMPORTANT: Exposing Services

Any new service that needs to be reachable from the internet MUST have a `.caddy` route snippet in `/etc/caddy/conf.d/`. See `services/CLAUDE.md` for the pattern.

## Usage

```bash
~/iapetus/attlas/base-setup/setup.sh
```

At the end, it prompts whether to also install services (ttyd, code-server).

8. Caddy web server with the base Caddyfile
9. Auto-updates Cloudflare DNS to point `attlas.uk` to the VM's external IP

## Dashboard Server (alive-server)

The dashboard is a Go binary at `alive-server/main.go`. It handles:
- Google OAuth2 login (`/oauth2/login`, `/oauth2/callback`)

**Do NOT add service routes to the base Caddyfile.** Services add their own routes by dropping `.caddy` snippet files into `/etc/caddy/conf.d/`. The base Caddyfile imports them via `import /etc/caddy/conf.d/*.caddy`.

## IMPORTANT: Exposing Services

Any new service that needs to be reachable from the internet MUST have a `.caddy` route snippet in `/etc/caddy/conf.d/`. See `services/CLAUDE.md` for the pattern.

## Usage

```bash
~/iapetus/attlas/base-setup/setup.sh
```

At the end, it prompts whether to also install services (ttyd, code-server).

# Services

Browser-accessible services that run behind the Caddy gateway.

## Current Services

- **Cloud Terminal** (`/terminal`) — [ttyd](https://github.com/tsl0922/ttyd) on port 7681
- **Cloud VS Code** (`/code`) — [code-server](https://github.com/coder/code-server) on port 8080
- **OpenClaw** — AI agent daemon (no web UI)

## Usage

```bash
~/iapetus/attlas/services/install.sh
```

This presents a menu of available services. Select which ones to install, or `a` for all.

## Adding a New Service

1. Create `install-myservice.sh` (second line comment = menu description)
2. Install binary + create systemd unit + copy `.caddy` snippet to `/etc/caddy/conf.d/`
3. Create `myservice.caddy` with the reverse proxy route
4. It will auto-appear in the install menu

**Without a `.caddy` snippet in `/etc/caddy/conf.d/`, a web service will NOT be reachable from the browser.**

file.content:
# services

Browser-accessible services running behind the Caddy gateway.

## Current Services

| Service | Script | Port | URL Path | Description |
|---------|--------|------|----------|-------------|
| terminal | `install-terminal.sh` | 7681 | /terminal | Web terminal (ttyd, zsh with dotfiles) |
| code-server | `install-code-server.sh` | 8080 | /code | VS Code in the browser |
| openclaw | `install-openclaw.sh` | 18789 | /openclaw | AI agent daemon (Telegram, Brave, Anthropic) |
| diary | `install-diary.sh` | — | /diary | Hugo-powered project diary (static site) |

## How install.sh Works

`install.sh` is a menu-based installer. It discovers all `install-*.sh` scripts in this directory and presents them as options. You can install individual services or all at once.

## IMPORTANT: Adding a New Service

Every service that needs to be reachable from the internet **MUST** have a Caddy route snippet. Without it, the service is only accessible on localhost.

To add a new service:

1. Create `install-myservice.sh` — the second comment line becomes the menu description
2. Inside the script:
   - Install the binary (with idempotency guard: `if ! command -v ... &>/dev/null`)
   - Create a systemd unit
   - **Copy a `.caddy` route snippet to `/etc/caddy/conf.d/`**
3. Create `myservice.caddy` in this directory:
   ```caddyfile
   handle /myservice* {
       reverse_proxy localhost:PORT
   }
   ```
4. `install.sh` will auto-discover the new `install-myservice.sh` and show it in the menu
5. Caddy is reloaded automatically after all selected services are installed

## Usage

```bash
~/iapetus/attlas/services/install.sh
```

input.old_string:
## Usage

```bash
~/iapetus/attlas/services/install.sh
```

This presents a menu of available services. Select which ones to install, or `a` for all.

## Adding a New Service

1. Create `install-myservice.sh` (second line comment = menu description)
2. Install binary + create systemd unit + copy `.caddy` snippet to `/etc/caddy/conf.d/`
3. Create `myservice.caddy` with the reverse proxy route
4. It will auto-appear in the install menu

**Without a `.caddy` snippet in `/etc/caddy/conf.d/`, a web service will NOT be reachable from the browser.**
input.new_string:
## Usage

Run **as root**; each `install-*.sh` writes a systemd unit under
`/etc/systemd/system/` and drops a `.caddy` snippet into
`/etc/caddy/conf.d/`:

```bash
sudo bash /home/agnostic-user/iapetus/attlas/services/install.sh
```

This presents a menu of available services. Select which ones to install, or `a` for all.

## Service user contract

Each `install-*.sh` hard-defaults the service user, but respects these env var overrides:

- `SERVICE_USER` — the system user the service runs as. Defaults:
  - ttyd, code-server, diary → `agnostic-user` (login user, backs browser shells / owns the repo)
  - openclaw → `openclaw-svc` (system nologin user with state under `/var/lib/openclaw/`)
- `SERVICE_STATE_DIR` — openclaw only. Defaults to `/var/lib/openclaw/`.

`alive-server.service` is installed by `base-setup/setup.sh`, not by any `services/install-*.sh`, and runs as its own dedicated `alive-svc` user.

## Adding a New Service

1. Create `install-myservice.sh` (second line comment = menu description). Must refuse to run unless invoked as root.
2. Install binary + create systemd unit (with explicit `User=${SERVICE_USER}`) + copy `.caddy` snippet to `/etc/caddy/conf.d/`.
3. Create `myservice.caddy` with the reverse proxy route.
4. It will auto-appear in the install menu.

**Without a `.caddy` snippet in `/etc/caddy/conf.d/`, a web service will NOT be reachable from the browser.**

1. Create `install-myservice.sh` — the second comment line becomes the menu description
2. Inside the script:
   - Install the binary (with idempotency guard: `if ! command -v ... &>/dev/null`)
   - Create a systemd unit
   - **Copy a `.caddy` route snippet to `/etc/caddy/conf.d/`**
3. Create `myservice.caddy` in this directory:
   ```caddyfile
   handle /myservice* {
       reverse_proxy localhost:PORT
   }
   ```
4. `install.sh` will auto-discover the new `install-myservice.sh` and show it in the menu
5. Caddy is reloaded automatically after all selected services are installed

## Usage

```bash
~/iapetus/attlas/services/install.sh
```

lines: +- `install-terminal.sh`, `install-code-server.sh`, `install-diary.sh` default to `SERVICE_USER=agnostic-user` and `User=agnostic-user` in the systemd unit. Browser shells / editors thus land in the login user's home.

+- `install-openclaw.sh` defaults to `SERVICE_USER=openclaw-svc` (a nologin system user) with `SERVICE_STATE_DIR=/var/lib/openclaw` and sets `Environment=HOME=${SERVICE_STATE_DIR}` on the unit, so openclaw finds its state at `/var/lib/openclaw/.openclaw/`.
+- `alive-server.service` itself is installed by `base-setup/setup.sh` (not here) and runs as the dedicated `alive-svc` user.

Split VM into agnostic-user + dedicated system service users

Removes the personal `condecopedro` login user from every entry point
and replaces it with a role-based account split so the VM can be
rebuilt from scratch without any hardcoded human identity.

New user topology:
  - agnostic-user    login (/bin/zsh, NOPASSWD sudo), owns the iapetus

                     and the dotfiles-sync timer.
  - alive-svc        system (nologin, no home), runs alive-server with

  - openclaw-svc     system (nologin, no home), runs openclaw-gateway
                     with state under /var/lib/openclaw/.

Layout on the VM now mirrors the laptop:
  /home/agnostic-user/iapetus/attlas
  /home/agnostic-user/iapetus/dotfiels

infra/singlevm-setup:
  - vm.tf enables OS Login (enable-oslogin=TRUE metadata), so
    `gcloud compute ssh` no longer auto-creates per-laptop phantom
    users matching the operator's local username.
  - startup.sh creates all three accounts and clones attlas into
    the agnostic workspace; everything else is deferred to base-setup.
  - variables.tf / terraform.tfvars / outputs.tf drop the vm_user
    variable entirely.

base-setup/setup.sh rewritten to run as root:
  - Provisions /var/lib/alive-server/ and seeds the OAuth config
    from Secret Manager before first start.
  - Writes alive-server.service with User=alive-svc, StateDirectory,
    and Environment=HOME/ATTLAS_DIR/CLAUDE_JSON_PATH set explicitly.
  - Builds the Go binary via `sudo -u agnostic-user` so on-disk
    ownership matches the repo.
  - Runs the dotfiels installer as agnostic-user to wire the
    home-level symlinks.

base-setup/alive-server/main.go gains two small env-var overrides
(backward compatible, fall back to $HOME/... if unset):
  - ATTLAS_DIR overrides filepath.Join(home,"attlas") for locating
    services/install-*.sh, uninstall scripts, and diary/public.
  - CLAUDE_JSON_PATH overrides the .claude.json location used by
    the cosmetic "is Claude logged in" indicator.

services/install-*.sh all now require root, accept SERVICE_USER (and
for openclaw, SERVICE_STATE_DIR) as env-var overrides, and emit
systemd units with explicit User= instead of $(whoami). Defaults:
agnostic-user for ttyd/code-server/diary, openclaw-svc for openclaw.

Docs (README.md / CLAUDE.md across infra, base-setup, services)
updated to describe the new user topology, the required sudo
invocation, and the SERVICE_USER contract.
EOF

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Linux-specific dotfiles setup. Sourced by install.sh.

echo "Installing packages..."
sudo apt-get update -qq
sudo apt-get install -y -qq zsh tmux python3 curl git

# dotfiles-sync: daily poll of origin via systemd user timer.
echo ""
echo "Installing dotfiles-sync service..."
chmod +x "$DOTFILES_DIR/systemd/dotfiles-sync.sh"
mkdir -p "$HOME/.config/systemd/user"
link "$DOTFILES_DIR/systemd/dotfiles-sync.service" "$HOME/.config/systemd/user/dotfiles-sync.service"
link "$DOTFILES_DIR/systemd/dotfiles-sync.timer" "$HOME/.config/systemd/user/dotfiles-sync.timer"

# Enable lingering so the user timer fires without an active login session.
if ! loginctl show-user "$USER" 2>/dev/null | grep -q 'Linger=yes'; then
  sudo loginctl enable-linger "$USER"
fi

systemctl --user daemon-reload
systemctl --user enable --now dotfiles-sync.timer
echo "  dotfiles-sync.timer enabled"

[Unit]
Description=Daily dotfiles sync
Documentation=https://github.com/TC-AVNP/dotfiels

[Timer]
# Run shortly after boot (if missed while off) and once per day.
OnBootSec=5min
OnCalendar=daily
# Catch up if the machine was off when the scheduled run was due.
Persistent=true
# Jitter so multiple machines don't hammer GitHub at the same instant.
RandomizedDelaySec=10min
Unit=dotfiles-sync.service

[Install]
WantedBy=timers.target

Plan for the dotfiels updates:
- Rewrite `systemd/dotfiles-sync.service` as a **system** unit with `User=agnostic-user` and an absolute `ExecStart` path (no `%h` — that resolves to `/root` for system units).
- Keep `systemd/dotfiles-sync.timer` as-is.
- Rewrite `install-linux.sh`: when the calling user is `agnostic-user`, install the units to `/etc/systemd/system/` via `sudo` (agnostic-user has NOPASSWD sudo on the VM). For any other Linux user, skip systemd — they can still use `dotfiles-sync.sh` manually.

input.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Linux-specific dotfiles setup. Sourced by install.sh.

echo "Installing packages..."
sudo apt-get update -qq
sudo apt-get install -y -qq zsh tmux python3 curl git

# dotfiles-sync: system-level systemd timer that pulls origin once a day.
#
# This is installed at /etc/systemd/system/ with User=agnostic-user baked
# into the unit, so it only makes sense on the attlas VM where that login
# user exists. On a Linux developer laptop (where the current user is not
# agnostic-user), skip the systemd install — dotfiles-sync.sh can still be
# run manually.
if [[ "${USER:-}" == "agnostic-user" ]]; then
  echo ""
  echo "Installing system-level dotfiles-sync..."
  chmod +x "$DOTFILES_DIR/systemd/dotfiles-sync.sh"
  sudo install -m 644 "$DOTFILES_DIR/systemd/dotfiles-sync.service" /etc/systemd/system/dotfiles-sync.service
  sudo install -m 644 "$DOTFILES_DIR/systemd/dotfiles-sync.timer" /etc/systemd/system/dotfiles-sync.timer
  sudo systemctl daemon-reload
  sudo systemctl enable --now dotfiles-sync.timer
  echo "  dotfiles-sync.timer enabled (system-level, running as agnostic-user)"
else
  echo ""
  echo "Skipping dotfiles-sync systemd install (not running as agnostic-user)."
  echo "To auto-sync manually: $DOTFILES_DIR/systemd/dotfiles-sync.sh"
fi

    38→# Source of truth: $DOTFILES_DIR/claude/memory
    39→# Symlinked into each Claude project memory dir so memory is shared across
    40→# projects and syncs via git across laptops (any username, any OS).
    41→#

file.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Dotfiles setup — detects OS and runs shared + platform-specific config.
# Safe to re-run.
set -euo pipefail

DOTFILES_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"

link() {
  local src="$1" dst="$2"
  if [[ -L "$dst" ]]; then
    rm "$dst"
  elif [[ -e "$dst" ]]; then
    echo "  backing up $dst → ${dst}.bak"
    mv "$dst" "${dst}.bak"
  fi
  ln -s "$src" "$dst"
  echo "  $dst → $src"
}

export -f link
export DOTFILES_DIR

OS="$(uname -s)"
echo "Detected OS: $OS"
echo ""

# --- Shared ---
echo "Installing shared dotfiles..."

# Zsh
link "$DOTFILES_DIR/zsh/.zshrc" "$HOME/.zshrc"

# Claude
mkdir -p "$HOME/.claude"
link "$DOTFILES_DIR/claude/settings.json" "$HOME/.claude/settings.json"

# Claude memory — shared across projects and laptops.
# Source of truth: $DOTFILES_DIR/claude/memory
# Symlinked into each Claude project memory dir so memory is shared across
# projects and syncs via git across laptops (any username, any OS).
#
# Add new entries as paths relative to $HOME. The encoded name that Claude
# Code expects under ~/.claude/projects/ is computed at install time, so the
# same dotfiles work on any laptop regardless of username.
mkdir -p "$DOTFILES_DIR/claude/memory"
CLAUDE_MEMORY_PROJECT_PATHS=(
  "$HOME/iapetus/attlas"
)
for proj_path in "${CLAUDE_MEMORY_PROJECT_PATHS[@]}"; do
  # Claude Code encodes absolute project paths by replacing / and . with -
  encoded="$(echo "$proj_path" | sed 's|[/.]|-|g')"
  mkdir -p "$HOME/.claude/projects/$encoded"
  link "$DOTFILES_DIR/claude/memory" "$HOME/.claude/projects/$encoded/memory"
done

# Tmux
link "$DOTFILES_DIR/tmux/tmux.conf" "$HOME/.tmux.conf"

# --- Platform-specific ---
case "$OS" in
  Darwin)
    echo ""
    echo "Running macOS setup..."
    source "$DOTFILES_DIR/install-macos.sh"
    ;;
  Linux)
    echo ""
    echo "Running Linux setup..."
    source "$DOTFILES_DIR/install-linux.sh"
    ;;
  *)
    echo "Unknown OS: $OS — skipping platform-specific setup."
    ;;
esac

echo ""
echo "Done. Open a new terminal to pick up changes."

content.text: All clean. Committing and pushing dotfiels.

input.command:
set -e
cd /Users/kerima.weller/iapetus/dotfiels
git status --short
echo "---"
git add -A
git -c user.name="Pedro Condeço" -c user.email="TC-AVNP@users.noreply.github.com" commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
Move dotfiles-sync to system-level systemd units

The sync timer is only meaningful on the attlas VM (where there's
an always-on login user that owns the repo). It was previously
installed as user-level units requiring loginctl linger, which
doesn't fit the new VM layout where the repo is owned by the
role-based `agnostic-user` account.

Rewrite the unit as a SYSTEM unit pinned to User=agnostic-user
with an absolute ExecStart path (`%h` resolves to /root for system
units, so it can't be used here). install-linux.sh detects when
it's being run by agnostic-user and `sudo`-installs the units into
/etc/systemd/system/; for any other Linux user (e.g. a dev laptop)
it skips the systemd install and points at the manual script path.
EOF
)"
git push origin master

Good news — the plan shows **1 to add, 1 to destroy** (a VM replacement) *without* me needing `terraform taint`. The combination of the new `metadata = { enable-oslogin }` block and the rewritten `startup.sh` both force-replace the instance automatically. That's cleaner than tainting.

The static IP is a separate resource and stays put. The boot disk goes with the VM (auto_delete=true), which is what we want — fully fresh state.

content.text:
Base setup completed successfully! Dashboard is responding on `localhost:3000`, Cloudflare DNS updated to `34.62.185.156`, all services running.

**Answering your question first (since it's genuinely interesting and the answer isn't what you might think):**

I actually **didn't taint**. I ran `terraform plan` and discovered Terraform *automatically* wanted to destroy and recreate the VM because the new code changed two force-replace attributes on `google_compute_instance`:
1. `metadata = { enable-oslogin = "TRUE" }` (new block)
2. `metadata_startup_script` (the body of `startup.sh` changed)

Both of those are marked `ForceNew: true` in the Google provider, so any change to them forces a replacement. `terraform plan` said "must be replaced" and the apply followed through. No taint needed.

**Now the real question: why destroy-then-create instead of create-then-destroy?**

Three hard constraints make create-then-destroy genuinely difficult here (not impossible, but significantly more complex):

1. **The static IP is a singleton resource**. `google_compute_address.vm_static_ip` can only be attached to one VM at a time. To have the new VM come up before the old one is destroyed, you'd need either (a) a second static IP temporarily, or (b) detach-reattach gymnastics. Terraform can express (a) with `create_before_destroy = true` and a conditional second IP, but it's not free — you pay for the extra IP, and DNS has to flip.

2. **VM names must be unique in a zone**. `simple-zombie` can't exist twice. So `create_before_destroy` would need to auto-generate a unique name for the replacement, and something else would have to rename it back or flip DNS to the temp name. Either way, more moving parts.

3. **DNS + Let's Encrypt certs**. `attlas.uk` points at the IP. During any create-then-destroy cycle, you'd have two VMs serving the same domain, and whichever one Cloudflare routes to (via a DNS flip) is the one Let's Encrypt issues a cert for. Juggling HTTP-01 challenge validation while both boxes answer on :80 is fiddly.

The Terraform pattern that *does* work for create-then-destroy is:
```hcl
resource "google_compute_instance" "vm" {
  lifecycle {
    create_before_destroy = true
  }
  # …
}
```
…combined with a VM name that includes a random suffix, and splitting the static IP so each VM has its own. That's 3-4 extra lines and a rename. The *net benefit* would be zero downtime (vs. ~4-5 minutes in my approach: ~1 min for destroy, ~1 min for create, ~3 min for `base-setup` to run).

**The reason I didn't propose it:** your VM is a dev/project box, not a production service. 4 minutes of downtime during a one-time migration is cheap, and the extra terraform complexity lives forever after. If zero-downtime matters enough to justify the ongoing complexity, we can switch — it's a small, localized change to `vm.tf` and a new terraform resource for a second IP. Tell me and I'll wire it up.

**Also genuinely relevant to your "prove it works from scratch" requirement:** destroy-then-create is actually *better* for that goal. A create-then-destroy would leave bits of the old VM running in parallel during the test, which means if anything from the old state leaked into the new VM (via DNS, shared IP, etc.), the test would pass but not actually prove the new VM is self-sufficient. Destroy-then-create is the strictest possible test: "can a fresh VM with nothing running bring itself up from cold?".

---

Continuing with the remaining work. Task #13 is done. Starting task #14 — installing the browser services and end-to-end verification.

gcloud compute ssh simple-zombie --zone=europe-west1-b --command='
echo "=== 1. systemd status summary ==="
for svc in alive-server ttyd code-server openclaw-gateway caddy dotfiles-sync.timer; do
  state=$(systemctl is-active "$svc" 2>&1)
  printf "  %-24s %s\n" "$svc" "$state"
done

echo ""
echo "=== 2. systemd units — running as the right user ==="
for svc in alive-server ttyd code-server openclaw-gateway; do
  user=$(systemctl show "$svc" -p User --value)
  printf "  %-24s User=%s\n" "$svc" "$user"
done

echo ""
echo "=== 3. localhost ports ==="
for port_info in "3000 alive-server" "7681 ttyd" "8080 code-server" "18789 openclaw"; do
  port=${port_info% *}; name=${port_info#* }
  if ss -tln | grep -q ":${port} "; then
    printf "  %-24s :%s listening ✓\n" "$name" "$port"
  else
    printf "  %-24s :%s NOT listening ✗\n" "$name" "$port"
  fi
done

echo ""
echo "=== 4. /var/lib state dirs ==="
sudo ls -la /var/lib/alive-server /var/lib/openclaw 2>&1 | head -20

echo ""
echo "=== 5. agnostic-user dotfiles ==="
sudo ls -la /home/agnostic-user/.zshrc /home/agnostic-user/.tmux.conf /home/agnostic-user/.claude/settings.json 2>&1

echo ""
echo "=== 6. claude memory symlink ==="
sudo ls -la /home/agnostic-user/.claude/projects/-home-agnostic-user-iapetus-attlas/memory 2>&1
sudo cat /home/agnostic-user/.claude/projects/-home-agnostic-user-iapetus-attlas/memory/MEMORY.md 2>&1 | head -10

echo ""
echo "=== 7. dotfiles-sync next run ==="
systemctl list-timers dotfiles-sync.timer --no-pager 2>&1 | head -5

echo ""
echo "=== 8. Caddy reachable (HTTPS) ==="
curl -s -o /dev/null -w "  https://attlas.uk/ → %{http_code}\n" https://attlas.uk/
curl -s -o /dev/null -w "  https://attlas.uk/diary/ → %{http_code}\n" https://attlas.uk/diary/
curl -s -o /dev/null -w "  https://attlas.uk/oauth2/login → %{http_code}\n" https://attlas.uk/oauth2/login

echo ""
echo "=== 9. confirm phantom users absent ==="
id condecopedro 2>&1 | head -1 || echo "condecopedro: absent ✓"
id kerima.weller 2>&1 | head -1 || echo "kerima.weller: absent ✓"
' 2>&1 | tail -80

content.content: Updated task #14 status

content.text:
---

## On your new request: "turn this into a Terraform module with multiple VMs round-robining on `attlas.uk`"

This is a real architectural shift, not a trivial rename. Let me lay out what it actually entails so we can scope it properly before I touch any code.

### What's easy
1. **Moving `singlevm-setup/` into `modules/attlas-vm/`** is mechanical. Inputs become `vm_name`, `zone`, `machine_type`, `disk_size`, `disk_image`, `attlas_repo`. Outputs become `vm_name`, `external_ip`, `instance_id`. The module creates: static IP (`google_compute_address`), the VM instance itself. That's it.
2. **A root config that instantiates the module `for_each`** over a map of VM specs — also straightforward:
   ```hcl
   module "vms" {
     source   = "./modules/attlas-vm"
     for_each = var.vms
     vm_name  = each.key
     zone     = each.value.zone
     # ...
   }
   ```
3. **Shared resources** (firewall rules, Secret Manager IAM bindings) stay at the root level and apply to all VMs via tags or service account patterns.

### What's genuinely hard — design decisions you need to make

**A. Session cookie consistency across backends.**
`alive-server` currently auto-generates its HMAC session secret at `/var/lib/alive-server/.attlas-session-secret` on first start (line 112 of main.go). If VM #1 and VM #2 generate *different* secrets, a user who OAuth-logs-in via VM #1 gets a session cookie that **fails to verify on VM #2**. DNS round-robin will hit different backends, so every other request would appear unauthenticated.

Three ways to fix:
- **(A1) Shared session secret in Secret Manager.** Create a new secret (e.g. `attlas-session-secret`), have `base-setup/setup.sh` fetch it and write to `/var/lib/alive-server/.attlas-session-secret` instead of letting alive-server auto-generate. Small code change to alive-server (read from the file if it exists, don't overwrite). **Recommended.**
- **(A2) Sticky sessions via Cloudflare.** Use a Cloudflare Worker or Load Balancer with session affinity. Overkill for this scale and adds Cloudflare-specific complexity.
- **(A3) Stateless JWTs with a shared signing key.** Rewrite the session layer. Biggest change, least needed.

**B. DNS updates and round-robin.**
Currently `base-setup/setup.sh` imperatively POSTs to the Cloudflare API to update a single A record for `attlas.uk` to point at *this* VM's IP. In a multi-VM world each VM overwrites the previous one — last-one-wins. Options:
- **(B1) Move DNS entirely into Terraform**, using the Cloudflare provider. Create N A records, one per module instance, all pointing at `attlas.uk`. Cloudflare serves these in round-robin by default. `base-setup/setup.sh` stops touching DNS. **Recommended.**
- **(B2) Keep imperative updates, have each VM add its IP to a multi-valued record.** Requires Cloudflare API gymnastics (read existing records, add if missing, remove when VM is destroyed). Not idempotent, hard to garbage-collect.
- **(B3) Cloudflare Load Balancer.** Pay for Cloudflare LB. Overkill.

**C. Services that don't make sense multi-instance.**
Some of the current services aren't safe to run on multiple VMs simultaneously:
- **`openclaw-gateway`**: maintains a Telegram bot connection, has a SQLite task database, has device identity. Running two instances with the *same* config means two daemons racing each other for the same Telegram bot (they'll fight over message polling). You probably want openclaw on exactly one VM, not all of them. **Needs a per-VM flag in the root config: `run_openclaw = true` for one VM, `false` for others.**
- **`ttyd` and `code-server`**: per-VM shells and editors. Fine on every VM — they give you independent work environments, which is actually useful for testing multiple zones.
- **`alive-server`**: safe to run on all VMs (once shared session secret is in place).
- **`diary` (Hugo)**: safe everywhere — read-only static site, each VM builds its own copy from git. Eventual consistency via `dotfiles-sync.timer` + manual attlas pulls.
- **Cloudflare DNS updater**: already discussed — move to Terraform.

**D. OAuth2 callback URLs.**
`attlas-server-config` (in Secret Manager) has allowed redirect URIs for Google OAuth. Multi-VM is fine as long as they all serve the *same* domain name (`attlas.uk`). They already do, so no change needed here.

**E. Let's Encrypt cert issuance.**
Each Caddy instance independently requests a cert for `attlas.uk`. Let's Encrypt is fine with multiple certs for the same name, but there are **rate limits** (50 certs/week/domain). If you're provisioning VMs frequently, this could bite. The fix is to either:
- Use Cloudflare's Origin CA certs (unlimited, but require Cloudflare trust in Caddy),
- Share Caddy's storage dir across VMs (complex, involves Redis/NFS),
- Or just accept it — 50 VMs/week is a lot.

**F. Terraform state for the root config.**
The current `terraform.tfstate` is committed to git. That's fine for a single-VM singleton but risky with multiple VMs because concurrent `terraform apply` from two laptops would corrupt state. Fix: move to **GCS remote backend** (a `gs://` bucket with state locking via GCS object generation). This is a one-time backend change.

### My recommended target shape

```
attlas/infra/
├── modules/
│   └── attlas-vm/                # reusable module
│       ├── main.tf               # static IP + google_compute_instance
│       ├── variables.tf          # vm_name, zone, machine_type, etc.
│       ├── outputs.tf            # external_ip, instance_name
│       └── startup.sh            # templated, same as today
├── root/                         # or "fleet/" or "env/prod/"
│   ├── main.tf                   # module "vms" { for_each = var.vms ... }
│   ├── firewall.tf               # shared firewall rules
│   ├── secrets.tf                # shared Secret Manager IAM
│   ├── dns.tf                    # NEW: cloudflare_record per VM, round-robin
│   ├── backend.tf                # NEW: GCS remote state
│   ├── variables.tf              # var.vms map
│   └── terraform.tfvars          # { simple-zombie = {zone="europe-west1-b",...}, ... }
```

Plus one new secret (`attlas-session-secret`), a small code change to `alive-server` to read its secret from a file without regenerating, and removing the DNS update block from `base-setup/setup.sh`.

### What I need from you before starting

1. **Session secret approach** — A1 (shared via Secret Manager + code change), or something else? (I strongly recommend A1.)
2. **DNS approach** — B1 (Terraform + Cloudflare provider, move DNS out of `base-setup`), or keep the imperative updates? (Strongly recommend B1.)
3. **Openclaw placement** — should there be a `run_openclaw = true` flag per VM, defaulting to `false`, with exactly one VM in the map having it `true`? Or do you want openclaw gone from this architecture entirely?
4. **Remote state** — move Terraform state to a GCS backend now, or defer?
5. **Initial fleet shape** — how many VMs to start with, and in which zones/regions? (e.g., `simple-zombie` in `europe-west1-b` + `tired-phoenix` in `us-central1-a`, or just `simple-zombie` for now as the only member of a `for_each` map so the plumbing is in place?)
6. **Scope for this pass** — do you want the module + multi-VM plumbing built *and* immediately deployed with 2+ VMs, or just the refactor (keeping the current 1-VM state but ready to scale)?

I'd also strongly recommend **treating this as a separate change after a pause** — the work you just asked for got done cleanly, the new VM is green, and rolling into a second big refactor without catching breath risks compounding mistakes. But I'll start immediately if you want.

**Task list is currently all-complete.** No active work until you decide on the above.

message.content: but why did we choose this /var/lib/alive-server/

content: why /var/lib

content.text:
Three reasons, tiered by importance:

**1. FHS convention + systemd native support.** `/var/lib/<service-name>/` is the standard Linux location for "variable state owned by a service". More importantly, systemd has first-class support: setting `StateDirectory=alive-server` in the unit file automatically:
- creates `/var/lib/alive-server/` if missing,
- chowns it to the `User=` of the service,
- sets mode 0700,
- (optionally) deletes it on `systemctl disable`.

So `/var/lib/alive-server/` isn't an arbitrary path — it's what systemd produces when you say `StateDirectory=alive-server`. Using any other path would require hand-rolling the create/chown/permissions logic in the install script.

**2. Ownership isolation from `agnostic-user`.** This is the one that actually matters for security. The two files in there are:
- `.attlas-server-config.json` — contains the **Google OAuth client secret** and the list of allowed emails
- `.attlas-session-secret` — the HMAC key that signs every session cookie

If these lived under `/home/agnostic-user/iapetus/attlas/` (or anywhere in `agnostic-user`'s home), then the user who backs `ttyd` and `code-server` would be able to read them. `agnostic-user` is *the* login user — whoever opens `/terminal` in the browser runs as that user. Reading those two files lets you mint valid session cookies for any allowed email. So they **must** be owned by a different user (`alive-svc`), and `alive-svc` has no home directory, so `/var/lib/alive-server/` is the only FHS-correct place they can live.

It's the same reason I pushed back on putting `/opt/iapetus/` at the top level earlier but kept `/var/lib/` for state: the repo is code (world-readable, no secrets in it), but the runtime state includes secrets that need tighter scoping than "whatever the interactive user can read".

**3. "Don't commit secrets to git" blast radius.** Even if the permissions argument weren't there, putting `.attlas-server-config.json` inside the attlas repo directory invites an accidental `git add .` + commit. Keeping state in `/var/lib/` is a physical separation that makes that class of mistake impossible.

The same logic applies to `/var/lib/openclaw/` — openclaw's `identity/device.json` has a device private key and `agents/main/agent/auth-profiles.json` has the Anthropic API key. Separate system user (`openclaw-svc`), separate state dir, separate ownership boundary from `alive-svc` as well (so if alive-server is compromised, openclaw's keys are still safe, and vice versa).

**tl;dr**: the repo goes in `/home/agnostic-user/iapetus/` because it's code and the interactive user owns it; secrets go in `/var/lib/<svc>/` because they're service-owned state that the interactive user must **not** be able to read.

message.content: why /var/lib

content.text:
Because it's the only one of the `/var/*` and `/etc/` options that actually matches what these files *are*. Let me line them all up:

| Candidate | FHS definition | systemd directive | Persists across reboot? | Fits? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `/etc/alive-server/` | Host-specific **configuration** edited by sysadmin | `ConfigurationDirectory=` | Yes | **No.** Neither file is hand-edited. `.attlas-server-config.json` is fetched from Secret Manager; `.attlas-session-secret` is auto-generated by the binary on first run. `/etc/` is "survives package uninstall because the sysadmin might have customized it" — that's the opposite of what we want for secrets. |
| `/var/cache/alive-server/` | Cache data the app **must be able to regenerate** | `CacheDirectory=` | Yes, but may be cleared | **No.** Regenerating `.attlas-session-secret` would invalidate every existing login cookie. And `.attlas-server-config.json` can't be regenerated at all — it lives in Secret Manager, fetched once. Not a cache. |
| `/var/spool/alive-server/` | Queued data awaiting processing (mail, print, cron) | *(none)* | Yes | **No.** Not a queue. |
| `/run/alive-server/` | Runtime data, **tmpfs, cleared on reboot** | `RuntimeDirectory=` | **No** | **No.** Session secret must survive reboots or every restart logs every user out. |
| `/var/log/alive-server/` | Logs | `LogsDirectory=` | Yes | **No.** Not logs — journald handles logging via `journalctl -u alive-server`. |
| **`/var/lib/alive-server/`** | **"State information. Persistent data modified by programs as they run."** | `StateDirectory=` | Yes | **Yes.** Both files are persistent state, modified/read by the service, not hand-edited config, not cache, not tmpfs. |

The FHS tree deliberately carves out these distinct buckets so sysadmins and tools like systemd know how to treat each. `/var/lib/` is the bucket for "stuff the service itself owns and writes and needs to keep across reboots" — which is exactly what `.attlas-session-secret` (auto-generated, must persist) and `.attlas-server-config.json` (fetched once, must persist) are.

**The practical consequence of picking the wrong bucket:**
- `/etc/` → some future cleanup script or package manager refuses to delete it on uninstall because "the sysadmin might have customized it", leaving stale OAuth secrets on the disk forever.
- `/var/cache/` → a disk-pressure cleanup tool (`systemd-tmpfiles`, `bleachbit`, etc.) might decide it's safe to wipe, killing all sessions.
- `/run/` → survives exactly one boot. Reboot the VM and everyone's logged out.
- `/var/lib/` → nothing touches it unless the service is explicitly uninstalled. Correct.

Plus the systemd ergonomics I mentioned: `StateDirectory=alive-server` in the unit file is the *one* line that creates `/var/lib/alive-server/` with the right owner and mode. No `install -d -o alive-svc ...` in a shell script, no drift between the unit file and reality, and `systemctl stop alive-server && systemctl disable alive-server` can optionally clean it up via `systemd-tmpfiles` rules if you ever want that.

tl;dr — it's not "`/var/lib/` was one of several reasonable choices and I picked that one." It's "`/var/lib/` is the one FHS bucket whose definition matches these files, and systemd has a one-line directive for it."

message.content: Ok, cool!   So, if i now ssh to the machine and start a claude code instance in the terminal will it have all the memory ?

content.text:
Yes — but only if you land as the right user *and* in the right directory. Here's the exact chain:

**Via `/terminal` in the browser** (the intended path):
1. Open `https://attlas.uk/terminal`. ttyd spawns zsh as `agnostic-user`, dropping you in `/home/agnostic-user/`.
2. `cd iapetus/attlas`
3. `claude`
4. On first run only, it'll prompt you to log in to your Claude account (OAuth flow).
5. After that, memory is loaded from `/home/agnostic-user/.claude/projects/-home-agnostic-user-iapetus-attlas/memory/`, which is the symlink `install.sh` created that points into `~/iapetus/dotfiels/claude/memory/`. All four feedback entries should appear in `MEMORY.md`.

**Via `gcloud compute ssh simple-zombie --zone=europe-west1-b`** (the CLI path):
1. You land as `condecopedro_gmail_com` (your OS Login IAM-derived username — a real user account auto-created on first SSH). This user has *no* dotfiles, *no* Claude memory symlinks, *no* settings.json link.
2. `sudo -iu agnostic-user` to become the agnostic user.
3. `cd ~/iapetus/attlas`
4. `claude`
5. Same first-run OAuth prompt, then memory works.

**The two things you must not forget:**
1. **Run as `agnostic-user`.** Claude Code looks up memory via `$HOME/.claude/projects/<encoded-cwd>/memory/`. If `$HOME` is `/home/condecopedro_gmail_com`, the lookup lands in a directory that doesn't exist and you get empty memory. The symlinks only exist under `agnostic-user`'s home.
2. **`cd` to `~/iapetus/attlas` before running `claude`.** The encoded-cwd calculation is based on your *current directory*, not your `$HOME`. Running `claude` from `~` gives an encoded path of `-home-agnostic-user`, which has no memory symlink. Running it from `~/iapetus/attlas` gives `-home-agnostic-user-iapetus-attlas`, which does. The daily `dotfiles-sync.timer` keeps the memory fresh from git.

**Two cosmetic gotchas worth knowing about:**
- The dashboard's "Is Claude logged in?" indicator at `https://attlas.uk/` will always say **No** even after you log in. That's because `alive-server` runs as `alive-svc` and the `CLAUDE_JSON_PATH=/home/agnostic-user/.claude.json` file is mode `600` owned by `agnostic-user` — alive-svc physically can't read it to check. We decided earlier this was acceptable (cosmetic only, non-blocking). Fix would be a group-read ACL or a sidecar status file, for a future pass.
- You'll also need to run `claude` once under `agnostic-user` to complete the OAuth login — this writes `/home/agnostic-user/.claude.json`, which survives reboots but not VM recreation. A future `terraform taint + apply` would log you out.

So the tl;dr: **yes, memory works, just `cd ~/iapetus/attlas` as agnostic-user first.**

toolUseResult.originalFile:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Dotfiles setup — detects OS and runs shared + platform-specific config.
# Safe to re-run.
set -euo pipefail

DOTFILES_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"

link() {
  local src="$1" dst="$2"
  if [[ -L "$dst" ]]; then
    rm "$dst"
  elif [[ -e "$dst" ]]; then
    echo "  backing up $dst → ${dst}.bak"
    mv "$dst" "${dst}.bak"
  fi
  ln -s "$src" "$dst"
  echo "  $dst → $src"
}

export -f link
export DOTFILES_DIR

OS="$(uname -s)"
echo "Detected OS: $OS"
echo ""

# --- Shared ---
echo "Installing shared dotfiles..."

# Zsh
link "$DOTFILES_DIR/zsh/.zshrc" "$HOME/.zshrc"

# Claude
mkdir -p "$HOME/.claude"
link "$DOTFILES_DIR/claude/settings.json" "$HOME/.claude/settings.json"

# Claude memory — shared across projects and laptops.
# Source of truth: $DOTFILES_DIR/claude/memory
# Symlinked into each Claude project memory dir so memory is shared across
# projects and syncs via git across laptops (any username, any OS).
#
# Add new entries as paths relative to $HOME. The encoded name that Claude
# Code expects under ~/.claude/projects/ is computed at install time, so the
# same dotfiles work on any laptop regardless of username.
mkdir -p "$DOTFILES_DIR/claude/memory"
CLAUDE_MEMORY_PROJECT_PATHS=(
  "$HOME/iapetus/attlas"
)
for proj_path in "${CLAUDE_MEMORY_PROJECT_PATHS[@]}"; do
  # Claude Code encodes absolute project paths by replacing / and . with -
  encoded="$(echo "$proj_path" | sed 's|[/.]|-|g')"
  mkdir -p "$HOME/.claude/projects/$encoded"
  link "$DOTFILES_DIR/claude/memory" "$HOME/.claude/projects/$encoded/memory"
done

# Tmux
link "$DOTFILES_DIR/tmux/tmux.conf" "$HOME/.tmux.conf"

# --- Platform-specific ---
case "$OS" in
  Darwin)
    echo ""
    echo "Running macOS setup..."
    source "$DOTFILES_DIR/install-macos.sh"
    ;;
  Linux)
    echo ""
    echo "Running Linux setup..."
    source "$DOTFILES_DIR/install-linux.sh"
    ;;
  *)
    echo "Unknown OS: $OS — skipping platform-specific setup."
    ;;
esac

echo ""
echo "Done. Open a new terminal to pick up changes."

lines: # same dotfiles work on any laptop regardless of username.
# Claude Code encodes absolute project paths by replacing / and . with -

content.text:
Both machines converted:

- **Laptop** (`kerima.weller`): `~/.claude/projects/-Users-kerima-weller-iapetus/memory` → dotfiels; old `-iapetus-attlas` dir removed.
- **VM** (`agnostic-user`): `~/.claude/projects/-home-agnostic-user-iapetus/memory` → dotfiels; old `-iapetus-attlas` dir removed. All 4 memory entries readable.

**New usage pattern:**

```bash
# On the VM (via /terminal browser shell or gcloud ssh + sudo -iu agnostic-user):
cd ~/iapetus
claude
```

Claude Code now computes the encoded project name from cwd = `/home/agnostic-user/iapetus` → `-home-agnostic-user-iapetus`, finds the symlink, loads `MEMORY.md`, and you have all four feedback entries in context — regardless of whether you're about to touch attlas, dotfiels, or jump between them.

Same pattern on the laptop: `cd ~/iapetus && claude` gives you the same four memory entries from the same shared dotfiels source of truth. The daily `dotfiles-sync.timer` keeps the VM's copy in sync with whatever you commit from the laptop.

Diary entries still land in `iapetus/attlas/diary/content/…` per the `feedback_diary.md` rule — that path is relative to wherever you're running, not tied to the Claude project root.

message.content:
Two cosmetic gotchas worth knowing about:
  - The dashboard's "Is Claude logged in?" indicator at https://attlas.uk/ will always say No even after you log in.
  That's because alive-server runs as alive-svc and the CLAUDE_JSON_PATH=/home/agnostic-user/.claude.json file is mode 600
   owned by agnostic-user — alive-svc physically can't read it to check. We decided earlier this was acceptable (cosmetic
  only, non-blocking). Fix would be a group-read ACL or a sidecar status file, for a future pass.
  - You'll also need to run claude once under agnostic-user to complete the OAuth login — this writes
  /home/agnostic-user/.claude.json, which survives reboots but not VM recreation. A future terraform taint + apply would
  log you out.    this seems odd, i dont like.  Is there no way that we can log via the dashbaord such that when we /terminal cklaude is logged in?

content.content:
1→#!/usr/bin/env python3
     2→"""
     3→Helper that runs 'claude login' with a PTY and navigates the setup wizard.
     4→Communicates with the dashboard via files in /tmp/.
     5→"""
     6→
     7→import pty
     8→import os
     9→import select
    10→import time
    11→import sys
    12→import re
    13→import struct
    14→import fcntl
    15→import termios
    16→import traceback
    17→
    18→URL_FILE = "/tmp/claude-login-url"
    19→CODE_FILE = "/tmp/claude-login-code"
    20→RESULT_FILE = "/tmp/claude-login-result"
    21→LOG_FILE = "/tmp/claude-login-helper.log"
    22→
    23→log_f = open(LOG_FILE, "w")
    24→
    25→def log(msg):
    26→    ts = time.strftime("%H:%M:%S")
    27→    line = f"[{ts}] {msg}"
    28→    log_f.write(line + "\n")
    29→    log_f.flush()
    30→
    31→def read_all(fd, timeout=5):
    32→    buf = b""
    33→    deadline = time.time() + timeout
    34→    while time.time() < deadline:
    35→        r, _, _ = select.select([fd], [], [], 0.3)
    36→        if r:
    37→            try:
    38→                buf += os.read(fd, 8192)
    39→            except OSError:
    40→                break
    41→        elif buf:
    42→            break
    43→    return buf
    44→
    45→def clean(data):
    46→    return re.sub(rb"\x1b\[[0-9;?]*[a-zA-Z]|\x1b[()][AB012]|\x1b[=>]|\x1b\][^\x07]*\x07", b"", data).decode(errors="replace")
    47→
    48→def main():
    49→    # Clean up
    50→    for f in [URL_FILE, CODE_FILE, RESULT_FILE]:
    51→        try: os.remove(f)
    52→        except FileNotFoundError: pass
    53→
    54→    master_fd, slave_fd = pty.openpty()
    55→    winsize = struct.pack("HHHH", 30, 120, 0, 0)
    56→    fcntl.ioctl(slave_fd, termios.TIOCSWINSZ, winsize)
    57→
    58→    env = os.environ.copy()
    59→    env["BROWSER"] = "echo"
    60→    env["TERM"] = "xterm-256color"
    61→    env["COLUMNS"] = "120"
    62→    env["LINES"] = "30"
    63→
    64→    pid = os.fork()
    65→    if pid == 0:
    66→        os.close(master_fd)
    67→        os.setsid()
    68→        fcntl.ioctl(slave_fd, termios.TIOCSCTTY, 0)
    69→        os.dup2(slave_fd, 0)
    70→        os.dup2(slave_fd, 1)
    71→        os.dup2(slave_fd, 2)
    72→        if slave_fd > 2: os.close(slave_fd)
    73→        os.execvpe("claude", ["claude", "login"], env)
    74→
    75→    # Parent
    76→    os.close(slave_fd)
    77→    log(f"Started claude login (PID {pid})")
    78→
    79→    # Continuously read PTY and navigate the wizard
    80→    # Goal: reach the "paste code" screen
    81→    # Along the way, press Enter on theme picker, login method, etc.
    82→    buf = b""
    83→    entered = set()
    84→    url = None
    85→    triggers = ["theme", "text style", "login method", "select login", "subscription", "claude account"]
    86→
    87→    log("Navigating wizard to 'paste code' screen...")
    88→
    89→    deadline = time.time() + 90
    90→    while time.time() < deadline:
    91→        data = read_all(master_fd, 3)
    92→        if data:
    93→            buf += data
    94→            log(f"  [{len(buf)} bytes total] last read: {len(data)} bytes")
    95→
    96→        text = clean(buf).lower()
    97→
    98→        # Check if we reached the code input screen
    99→        if "paste code" in text or ("paste" in text and "code" in text):
   100→            log("  REACHED 'paste code' screen!")
   101→            break
   102→
   103→        # Press Enter on any wizard screen we recognize
   104→        matched = False
   105→        for trigger in triggers:
   106→            if trigger in text and trigger not in entered:
   107→                log(f"  found '{trigger}' — waiting 2s then pressing Enter")
   108→                time.sleep(2)
   109→                os.write(master_fd, b"\r")
   110→                entered.add(trigger)
   111→                log(f"  Enter pressed for '{trigger}'")
   112→                time.sleep(3)
   113→                # Read new data and APPEND (don't reset)
   114→                new_data = read_all(master_fd, 5)
   115→                buf += new_data
   116→                log(f"  after Enter: +{len(new_data)} bytes, total {len(buf)}")
   117→                matched = True
   118→                break
   119→
   120→        if not matched and not data:
   121→            log(f"  no data, waiting...")
   122→            time.sleep(2)
   123→
   124→    # Extract URL from accumulated output
   125→    # The URL may be wrapped across lines in the terminal output
   126→    # Strip all ANSI codes and whitespace, then find the URL
   127→    raw = buf.decode(errors="replace")
   128→    # Remove ANSI escape sequences
   129→    stripped = re.sub(r"\x1b\[[0-9;?]*[a-zA-Z]|\x1b[()][AB012]|\x1b[=>]|\x1b\][^\x07]*\x07", "", raw)
   130→    # Remove carriage returns AND newlines (URL wraps across lines at terminal width)
   131→    stripped = stripped.replace("\r", "").replace("\n", "")
   132→
   133→    # Find URL. After stripping ANSI + newlines, the URL runs into the next text.
   134→    # Known trailing text is "Pastecodehereifprompted>" or "Browserdidn'topen..."
   135→    # Simply cut at "Paste" or at the first uppercase letter after "state="
   136→    url_match = re.search(r"(https://claude\.com/[^\s]*|https://console\.anthropic\.com/[^\s]*)", stripped)
   137→    if url_match:
   138→        url = url_match.group(1)
   139→        # Cut at "Paste" if it got concatenated
   140→        for cut_word in ["Paste", "Browser", "Press", "Sign"]:
   141→            idx = url.find(cut_word)
   142→            if idx > 0:
   143→                url = url[:idx]
   144→                break
   145→    log(f"Extracted URL ({len(url) if url else 0} chars): ends with ...{url[-30:] if url else ''}")
   146→
   147→    if not url:
   148→        log(f"ERROR: No URL found. Clean text: {clean(buf)[-500:]}")
   149→        with open(RESULT_FILE, "w") as f:
   150→            f.write("ERROR: Could not find auth URL")
   151→        os.kill(pid, 9)
   152→        return
   153→
   154→    log(f"URL: {url[:80]}...")
   155→    with open(URL_FILE, "w") as f:
   156→        f.write(url)
   157→
   158→    # Wait for code
   159→    log("Waiting for code file...")
   160→    code = None
   161→    for i in range(300):
   162→        if os.path.exists(CODE_FILE):
   163→            with open(CODE_FILE) as f:
   164→                code = f.read().strip()
   165→            if code:
   166→                os.remove(CODE_FILE)
   167→                break
   168→        if i % 30 == 0:
   169→            log(f"  waiting... ({i}s)")
   170→            try: os.kill(pid, 0)
   171→            except ProcessLookupError:
   172→                log("ERROR: Process died")
   173→                with open(RESULT_FILE, "w") as f:
   174→                    f.write("ERROR: claude login exited")
   175→                return
   176→        time.sleep(1)
   177→
   178→    if not code:
   179→        log("ERROR: Timed out")
   180→        with open(RESULT_FILE, "w") as f:
   181→            f.write("ERROR: Timed out waiting for code")
   182→        os.kill(pid, 9)
   183→        return
   184→
   185→    # Type code
   186→    log(f"Typing code ({len(code)} chars)...")
   187→    for ch in code:
   188→        os.write(master_fd, ch.encode())
   189→        time.sleep(0.01)
   190→    time.sleep(0.5)
   191→    os.write(master_fd, b"\r")
   192→    log("Code submitted, handling post-login prompts...")
   193→
   194→    # Handle remaining prompts (security notice, trust folder)
   195→    time.sleep(5)
   196→    post_buf = read_all(master_fd, 10)
   197→    post_text = clean(post_buf).lower()
   198→    log(f"Post-login: {len(post_buf)} bytes")
   199→
